summary: Brendan Gregg discovered that loud noises can cause measurable disk latency in datacenter hard drives, initially stumbled upon while investigating latency caused by a missing screw on a drive bracket. The discovery was documented in a video demonstration and analyzed using Fishworks analytics. This connects to known research on non-repeatable runout (NRRO) in hard drives, where noise can interfere with disk head positioning.
key_points: Loud noises can cause measurable latency in spinning hard drives
Discovery originated from investigating a missing screw causing drive vibration
Fishworks analytics enabled visualization of the latency patterns
Non-repeatable runout (NRRO) is a known phenomenon caused by noise interference
money_quotes: This may seem silly, but it's not farfetched: Brendan actually made this discovery while exploring drive latency that he had seen in a lab machine due to a missing screw on a drive bracket.
As Jon notes, noise is a known cause of what is called a non-repeatable runout (NRRO) – though it's unclear if Brendan's shouting is exactly the kind of noise-induced NRRO that Jon had in mind…
key_insight: Physical phenomena like sound vibrations can have measurable effects on disk performance, and good observability tools can help uncover unexpected sources of system latency.
